stage1.customer_context: Hartwell & Co. is a commercial real estate firm where the Marketing department (led by VP Elena) and Research department (led by Director Sam) both run manual, high-volume document and information-request processes for brokers. Data already lives in structured systems (Postgres property database, research warehouse, demographics API) but assembly and status-tracking are entirely manual, creating bottlenecks and confidentiality risks tied to per-listing access restrictions.

workflows.name: Offering Memorandum (OM) Production
workflows.pain: Every listing requires a 30+ page branded document manually assembled in InDesign from photos, rent rolls, comps, demographics, and financials by a team of six; average turnaround is 5 business days across 340/year, and brokers complain constantly.

champion.name: Elena
champion.evidence: Offering memorandums are the bottleneck... My team of six produces them by hand in InDesign.

workflows.evidence_quote: Every listing needs one: property photos, rent rolls, comps, demographic data, financial summaries, all assembled into a branded 30-plus page document.

workflows.name: Listing Collateral Status Requests
workflows.pain: Brokers email or call marketing roughly 30 times a day asking where their collateral is in the production queue, pulling staff off actual production work.
systems: internal production queue (implied, no system of record)

champion.name: Elena
champion.evidence: Someone on my team answers roughly 30 of those a day instead of doing actual work.

workflows.evidence_quote: Brokers email or call marketing to ask where their collateral is in the queue.

workflows.name: Comparable Sales (Comp) Report Generation
workflows.pain: Brokers request comparable sales for pitches; two analysts manually run the same ~8 queries with different addresses, format results into a PDF, and send it back, consuming roughly half their week.

champion.name: Sam
champion.evidence: Two analysts spend maybe half their week on it. It is the same eight queries with different addresses.

workflows.evidence_quote: Brokers ask for comparable sales for a pitch, we run the query, format a PDF, send it back.

workflows.name: Proposal / Pitch Tracker
workflows.pain: Pitch and proposal tracking currently lives in a color-coded spreadsheet understood only by one senior coordinator, creating a single point of failure and no visibility for others.

champion.name: Marcus
champion.evidence: Proposal tracking for pitches, currently a color-coded spreadsheet that only our senior coordinator, Marcus, understands.

workflows.evidence_quote: Proposal tracking for pitches, currently a color-coded spreadsheet that only our senior coordinator, Marcus, understands.

workflows.name: Listing Photo Asset Requests
workflows.pain: Photo assets for listings are requested and shared via a shared drive with no organization or access control, described as chaotic.
workflows.evidence_quote: listing photo asset requests (shared drive chaos)

rationales.impact: 340 OMs/year tie up a six-person team for 5 business days each and are the direct source of chronic broker complaints, making this the highest revenue-adjacent bottleneck in the portfolio.
rationales.data_readiness: Source systems are named and structured (Postgres, research warehouse, demographics API) but final assembly happens in InDesign, meaning the workflow spans four disparate systems and a design tool that resists straightforward automation.
rationales.governance_simplicity: OMs bundle financials, rent rolls, and comps with per-listing confidentiality restrictions, so any automation must respect access controls, adding approval and security review layers.
rationales.champion_strength: Elena, VP Marketing, explicitly names this as her team's core bottleneck and owns the six-person production team, giving strong sponsorship and urgency.

rationales.impact: About 30 interruptions a day pull marketing staff off production work, so fixing it recovers meaningful capacity, though it doesn't directly touch revenue or error risk the way OM production does.
rationales.data_readiness: There is no named system of record for production status today, only email and phone, so a queue/status system would need to be built rather than connected to something existing.
rationales.governance_simplicity: Status information is low-sensitivity and internal, so this can ship with minimal approvals once a lightweight tracking layer exists.
rationales.champion_strength: Elena again identifies this as a direct drain on her team and is already motivated to fix it, making her a ready-made owner and internal reference.

rationales.impact: Two analysts spend roughly half their week running the same eight queries for broker pitches, so automation frees substantial skilled headcount tied directly to deal support.
rationales.data_readiness: The workflow lives entirely in one structured research data warehouse with a repeatable, well-defined query pattern, making it the most connectable and template-able process in the set.
rationales.governance_simplicity: Comp sales data is less sensitive than full financials or per-listing restricted materials and touches a single system, so it can move through review quickly.
rationales.champion_strength: Sam, Research Director, names this pain directly and quantifies the analyst time lost, indicating clear ownership and motivation to sponsor a pilot.

rationales.impact: The tracker prevents a single point of failure risk since only one coordinator understands it, but no volume or dollar impact is cited, so the upside is more about resilience than throughput.
rationales.data_readiness: The process currently lives in an ad hoc color-coded spreadsheet with no named external system to connect to, requiring the data model to be built from scratch.
rationales.governance_simplicity: This is a purely internal tracking tool with no confidential listing data, so it faces minimal governance friction to replace.
rationales.champion_strength: Marcus, the senior coordinator, is named and clearly invested since he is the single point of failure, but he lacks the department-level authority Elena or Sam have.

rationales.impact: Described only as 'shared drive chaos' with no volume, turnaround, or cost figures given, suggesting real but currently unquantified and likely lower-priority pain.
rationales.data_readiness: Assets sit in an unorganized shared drive with no structure, metadata, or access rules, requiring significant groundwork before any workflow could connect to it.
rationales.governance_simplicity: Photo assets intersect with the same per-listing confidentiality restrictions noted elsewhere, so access control work is needed even though the content itself is not highly sensitive.
rationales.champion_strength: No champion is named for this workflow, leaving no one accountable to push it forward or validate a pilot.

rollout.champion_moves: Have Sam publicly sponsor a scoped pilot covering just the top 3-4 most common comp queries against the research warehouse, producing an auto-formatted PDF for one active broker pitch to create an immediate before/after story.
rollout.proof_metric: Analyst hours spent per comp report drop from ~half a week per request pattern to near-instant generation, measured across the first 10-15 requests fulfilled through the new flow.

rollout.champion_moves: Use the comp-report win to bring Elena in as co-sponsor; start OM automation on the structured data feeds (Postgres, warehouse, demographics API) for one property type first while manually handling InDesign branding, and simultaneously stand up a simple shared status view fed by the same production data to kill the 30-a-day broker check-ins.
rollout.proof_metric: OM assembly time for the pilot property type drops from 5 business days toward 1-2 days, and broker status inquiries to marketing staff fall from ~30/day to near zero for piloted listings.

rollout.champion_moves: Bring Marcus in to co-design a structured replacement for his spreadsheet so the tracker is no longer a single point of failure, and use the momentum from OM/status wins to justify a governance pass on photo asset access control before rolling out an organized, permissioned asset workflow.
rollout.proof_metric: Pitch tracking becomes accessible to at least 3 additional team members beyond Marcus, and photo asset requests are resolved through a structured, access-controlled system with a documented reduction in ad hoc search/request time.

stage3.clark_prompt: Build an internal tool for the Research team that automates Comparable Sales (Comp) Report generation for brokers. Today two analysts manually re-run the same roughly eight queries against the @ResearchWarehouse for each broker request, then hand-format the results into a PDF and email it back, consuming about half their week. The tool should connect directly to @ResearchWarehouse as the single source of comp sales data (transaction records, addresses, sale prices, price per square foot, closing dates, property type). Build three views: (1) a Request Intake view where an analyst enters or pastes a broker's request (property address or list of addresses, deal context, requested comp radius/date range) and selects from the standard set of ~8 saved query templates; (2) a Report Preview view that runs those queries against @ResearchWarehouse, surfaces the results in an editable table so the analyst can remove outliers, adjust rounding, or add notes before finalizing; (3) a Report Library view showing past generated reports, who requested them, and when they were sent, so analysts and Sam can spot repeat requests or reuse recent pulls. The human-in-the-loop points are: an analyst must review and approve the pulled comp data in the Preview view before any PDF is generated or sent (no fully automated send-to-broker step), and Sam, as Research Director, should periodically review and approve changes to the underlying query templates so the standard 8 queries stay accurate as the data model evolves. Output should be a branded, consistently formatted PDF that mirrors the firm's current comp report layout. Before you build, give me a plan covering the data model for pulling from @ResearchWarehouse, the screens/interactions above, and where the analyst review checkpoint sits in the flow.

integrations.why: Analysts and Sam need query-building and template-editing access to run and maintain the ~8 standard comp queries against live warehouse data.
Front-line staff who intake broker requests need read-only access to trigger report generation without direct query access to the underlying warehouse.

rbac_notes.why: Needs to run queries, edit results in the Preview view, and generate/send final PDFs; this is the primary daily operator role.
Owns and approves changes to the standard query templates and reviews report volume/quality trends via the Report Library view.

rbac_notes.who: Marketing/brokerage staff submitting requests on behalf of brokers
rbac_notes.why: Needs to submit a comp request and view report status/history but should not have direct access to raw warehouse data or query editing.

knowledge_files.body:
# Research Warehouse Schema Guidance

- Comp sales data lives in the research data warehouse; treat it as the single source of truth for comp report queries.
- Core fields to expect: property address, sale price, sale date, price per square foot, property type, and square footage.
- The 8 standard comp queries should be treated as reusable templates, not ad hoc SQL rewritten per request; version and label them clearly (e.g., 'Comp-Query-01: Same submarket, last 12 months').
- Always parameterize queries by address/geography and date range rather than hardcoding values, since every request only varies these inputs.
- Do not join comp warehouse data with per-listing restricted marketing data (rent rolls, financials); those are governed separately and out of scope for this workflow.
- Flag any query returning zero or very sparse results back to the analyst rather than auto-generating a report, since sparse comps need manual judgment.
- When adding new query templates, document the business question they answer so future analysts understand intent, not just SQL.

# Comp Report Generation Workflow Pattern

- Flow: Intake request -> Run standard query templates -> Analyst reviews/edits results -> Generate PDF -> Send to broker -> Log in Report Library.
- Never skip the analyst review step; results must be checked for outliers or stale comps before a PDF is produced, even though queries run automatically.
- Each request should capture: requesting broker/deal context, target address(es), and which of the 8 templates were used, so patterns in demand are visible over time.
- The Report Library should be searchable by address and date so analysts can quickly check if a similar comp pull was done recently, reducing duplicate work.
- Turnaround target for this workflow is near-instant generation once a request is submitted, not same-day; design the intake-to-preview step to complete in seconds, not minutes.
- Template changes (adding/editing one of the 8 standard queries) should route to Sam for approval before going live, since these are shared across all analysts.

# Comp Report PDF Design Standards

- Generated PDFs should mirror the firm's existing comp report layout so brokers see no disruption in format from the manual process.
- Standard sections: cover header with requesting broker's name and date, comparable sales table, price-per-square-foot summary, and a notes/caveats section for analyst-added context.
- Keep tables sortable by sale date and price per square foot in the underlying data view, but the exported PDF should present them in a clean, print-ready static table.
- Include a visible 'Generated on [date] by [analyst]' footer for traceability, since these reports go directly to brokers and external-facing pitch materials.
- Avoid including any fields not part of the standard comp data set (no rent roll or financial data) to keep this workflow's output distinct from Offering Memorandum materials.

# Listing Data Confidentiality Notes

- Hartwell & Co. enforces per-listing access restrictions across property and deal data; not all staff can see all listings.
- Comp sales data in the research warehouse is generally lower-sensitivity than per-listing financials or rent rolls, but reports should still only include information the requesting broker is authorized to see.
- Do not blend comp warehouse data with restricted marketing materials (financial summaries, rent rolls) in any single automated workflow without an explicit governance review.
- When building tools that touch listing-adjacent data, default to least-privilege access and confirm with a department lead (Sam for Research, Elena for Marketing) before expanding visibility across teams.

metrics.metric: Analyst hours spent per comp report
metrics.target: Reduce from ~half a week (aggregate across 2 analysts) to near-instant generation per request
metrics.how_measured: Compare analyst time logs/self-reported hours before and after rollout across the first 10-15 requests fulfilled through the new flow.

metrics.target: From request submission to broker-ready PDF in under 10 minutes including analyst review
metrics.how_measured: Timestamp comparison between Intake submission and final PDF send logged in the Report Library.

metrics.metric: Requests fulfilled through new flow vs. manual process
metrics.target: 100% of new comp requests routed through the tool within first 30 days
metrics.how_measured: Count of reports generated in Report Library vs. Sam's estimate of total broker comp requests received.

metrics.target: Track and keep manual overrides/outlier removals visible, not eliminate them
metrics.how_measured: Percentage of generated Preview results that are edited before PDF generation, logged per report.

metrics.target: Zero unapproved changes to the 8 standard query templates
metrics.how_measured: Audit log of template edits cross-checked against Sam's approval records.
